title_en: Worship and Legal Hadiths in the Mustadrak
content_en:
The Mustadrak of al-Hakim is organized along the conventional topical lines of Islamic jurisprudence, beginning with the Quran and faith, moving through the acts of worship, and then covering the legal rulings of social life. This organization mirrors that of the two Sahihs in general structure, though al-Hakim does not always follow the same internal organization. The opening sections on the Quran — including traditions on its virtues, recitation, and proper relationship to the Muslim believer — are among the most cited portions of the Mustadrak, since they preserve important traditions not found in quite the same form in the canonical collections.

The purification and prayer sections contain a mixture of the valuable and the questionable. Al-Hakim authenticated several important traditions on prayer methodology and the conditions of valid prayer that supplemented the material in al-Bukhari and Muslim, and many of these received ad-Dhahabi's confirmation. Others in the same sections were flagged by ad-Dhahabi as weak or fabricated, requiring the reader to consult his critique before citing any specific narration with confidence.

The traditions on zakat, fasting, and pilgrimage in the Mustadrak are similarly mixed but contain valuable material. The pilgrimage sections are notable for the detail they provide on specific rites of hajj through chains that complement those in the primary Sahihs, and several rulings in Hanbali and Shafi'i fiqh find important support in Mustadrak traditions that passed ad-Dhahabi's scrutiny. The fasting sections include authenticated traditions on the night prayer of Ramadan (tarawih) and the Night of Power that have been cited by scholars across the legal schools.

The legal sections covering commercial transactions, marriage, divorce, and inheritance are valuable for the same reasons that the worship sections are: they supplement the canonical collections with additional authenticated chains for rulings that are well established in Islamic law. The critical user of the Mustadrak treats each section with the same discipline, verifying al-Hakim's authentication against ad-Dhahabi's review before relying on any specific tradition.
